children.name: Lacerta
children.description: True lizards of open habitats. The Sand Lizard is one of only two lizard species breeding in Sweden — males develop vivid green flanks in breeding season, transforming from drab brown to brilliant green over several weeks in spring. It requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying and is declining across its northern range.

children.name: Lacerta agilis
children.description: One of two lizard species native to Sweden; breeding males develop vivid green flanks; requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying; declining in northern range; Swedish populations are protected.

children.name: Lacerta viridis
children.description: Brilliant iridescent green; males have a blue throat in breeding season; largest lacertid in Europe at up to 40 cm.

children.name: Lacerta bilineata
children.description: Recently split from L. viridis; France and Iberia.

children.name: Lacerta strigata
children.description: A slender green lizard from the Caucasus, Iran and Turkmenistan; males display bright blue throats in spring.

children.name: Lacerta media
children.description: A Middle Eastern species found in Iran, Iraq and the Levant; prefers oak woodlands and rocky slopes.

children.name: Lacerta pamphylica
children.description: Restricted to the Mediterranean coast of Turkey; isolated population of green lizards with distinctive dorsal spotting.

children.name: Lacerta anatolica
children.description: Endemic to western Anatolia; inhabits maquis shrubland and rocky hillsides.

children.name: Lacerta cypernica
children.description: Endemic to Cyprus; the largest green lizard on the island; prefers forest edges.

children.name: Lacerta trilineata
children.description: A large green lizard of the Balkans; three yellow lines run the length of its body.

children.name: Zootoca
children.description: Cold-adapted viviparous lizards.

children.name: Zootoca vivipara
children.description: Sweden's most widespread reptile; found from southern Sweden to the Arctic fell; viviparous in most populations — gives birth to live young rather than laying eggs; a critical adaptation to cold northern climates.

children.name: Zootoca carniolica
children.description: A recently split species from the Z. vivipara complex; restricted to the Dinaric Alps of Slovenia and Croatia; lays eggs unlike northern populations.

children.name: Zootoca louisanti
children.description: A microendemic of the Pyrenees; separated from Z. vivipara by genetic analysis and ecological niche differences.

children.name: Zootoca monticola
children.description: A high-elevation form from the Alps; bears live young at altitudes above 2000 m.

children.name: Zootoca horvathi
children.description: A secretive species from the Dinaric karst; prefers limestone rock crevices.

children.name: Zootoca ozenae
children.description: A recently described viviparous lizard from the Slovenian Alps.

children.name: Podarcis
children.description: Diverse wall lizards.

children.name: Podarcis muralis
children.description: Extreme generalist; colonising northward in a warming climate; now established in UK, Belgium, Netherlands; the fastest-diversifying lizard in Europe.

children.name: Podarcis siculus
children.description: Classic study system in rapid parallel evolution following island introductions.

children.name: Podarcis lilfordi
children.description: Endemic to the Balearic Islands; many subspecies on separate islets; critically endangered by introduced predators.

children.name: Podarcis pityusensis
children.description: Endemic to Ibiza and Formentera; over 30 subspecies described, one per islet; highly variable colouration.

children.name: Podarcis carbonelli
children.description: A Portuguese endemic restricted to the Atlantic coast and Berlenga Island; threatened by dune habitat loss.

children.name: Podarcis bocagei
children.description: Found in northwestern Iberia; males have vivid green flanks and blue throat spots.

children.name: Podarcis guadarramae
children.description: A recently split species from the Iberian system; inhabits granite boulder fields.

children.name: Podarcis vaucheri
children.description: A North African and southern Spanish wall lizard; named after the naturalist.

children.name: Timon
children.description: Ocellated lizards.

children.name: Timon lepidus
children.description: Europe's largest lizard at up to 90 cm; brilliant blue ocelli along flanks; Iberian endemic; IUCN Near Threatened.

children.name: Timon tangitanus
children.description: A large lacertid endemic to Morocco and northwestern Algeria; vivid blue ocelli on flanks.

children.name: Timon pater
children.description: From semi-arid regions of Algeria and Tunisia; distinguished by more pronounced head scales than T. lepidus.

children.name: Timon princeps
children.description: Found in Iran, Turkey and Syria; the easternmost Timon species, inhabiting rocky mountain slopes.

children.name: Timon nevadensis
children.description: A recently split species from the Sierra Nevada of Spain; isolated high-mountain form.

children.name: Timon antiquus
children.description: Known from Pleistocene fossils across the Mediterranean; now restricted to relict populations.

children.name: Algyroides
children.description: Small secretive lizards of southern Europe with keeled dorsal scales; also known as keeled lizards.

children.name: Algyroides fitzingeri
children.description: A tiny lacertid from Corsica and Sardinia, found in shaded leaf litter; rarely more than 12 cm total length.

children.name: Algyroides nigropunctatus
children.description: From the western Balkans; males have a vivid blue throat and black-spotted flanks during breeding.

children.name: Algyroides moreoticus
children.description: Endemic to the Peloponnese peninsula; the least-known Algyroides; inhabits moist ravines and spring seeps.

children.name: Psammodromus
children.description: Fast, diurnal lizards of southwestern Europe and North Africa; prefer open sandy or scrub habitats.

children.name: Psammodromus algirus
children.description: The most common lacertid of Iberian scrub; lays 6-11 eggs; females show orange throat colour when gravid.

children.name: Psammodromus hispanicus
children.description: A small slender lizard of sandy soils in central and eastern Spain; very fast and difficult to catch.

children.name: Psammodromus edwardianus
children.description: A North African species from Morocco and Tunisia; inhabits coastal dunes and arid stony plains.

children.name: Psammodromus microdactylus
children.description: An Algerian and Moroccan species with reduced toe lamellae adapted to fine sand; secretive burrower.

children.name: Takydromus
children.description: Slender long-tailed lacertids of East and Southeast Asia; often called grass lizards for their preferred habitat.

children.name: Takydromus takydromoides
children.description: Endemic to Japan; tail can be up to three times body length; inhabits open grasslands and forest edges.

children.name: Takydromus sexlineatus
children.description: Widespread from India to Southeast Asia; six pale stripes run the length of its body; active in daytime.

children.name: Takydromus amurensis
children.description: The northernmost Takydromus; found in the Russian Far East, northeastern China and Korea.

children.name: Takydromus wolteri
children.description: Found in China, Korea and the Russian Far East; prefers moist grasslands near water bodies.

children.name: Darevskia
children.description: A large genus of parthenogenetic and bisexual rock lizards from the Caucasus and Middle East.

children.name: Darevskia armeniaca
children.description: A parthenogenetic species from Armenia and Georgia; all-female populations reproduce by cloning.

children.name: Darevskia saxicola
children.description: Found in the Caucasus mountains; a bisexual species of rocky limestone slopes.

children.name: Darevskia praticola
children.description: The westernmost Darevskia; found in the Balkans and Caucasus; inhabits damp grassy habitats.

children.name: Darevskia raddei
children.description: A robust rock lizard from Armenia, Azerbaijan and Iran.

children.name: Darevskia defilippii
children.description: A parthenogenetic species from northern Iran.

children.name: Iberolacerta
children.description: Small high-mountain lizards of the Iberian Peninsula; restricted to alpine habitats above treeline.

children.name: Iberolacerta monticola
children.description: A high-altitude species from the Cantabrian Mountains.

children.name: Iberolacerta cyrnensis
children.description: Endemic to Corsica; lives in granite boulder fields and alpine meadows.

children.name: Iberolacerta martinezricai
children.description: A critically endangered species restricted to a single mountain in the Pyrenees.

children.name: Iberolacerta bonnali
children.description: A Pyrenean endemic; one of the most cold-adapted reptiles in Europe.
